beats.narration_text: Hei — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to build a landing page with a strong visual identity. Before it writes a line of CSS, the frontend-design skill fires. The first thing it says: there are three looks every AI defaults to, and this brief probably does not want any of them. The skill positions itself as a design lead, not a template factory. Let me show you what it requires.

props.command: Build a landing page with a strong visual identity — distinctive typography, a considered palette, and one element I will not have seen elsewhere.
output: 3 AI design defaults blocked: cream+serif, dark+acid-green, broadsheet
2-pass process: design plan → critique → code (in that order)
Token system: 4–6 hex values · typefaces · ASCII wireframe · signature

beats.act: anatomy — trigger + principles + defaults to avoid
beats.narration_text: The skill covers five design principles. One: the hero is a thesis — open with the most characteristic thing in the subject's world, in whatever form makes sense. Not a gradient stat block unless that is truly the best choice. Two: typography carries personality — pair typefaces deliberately for this brief, not the same families you reach for on any other project. Three: structure is information — numbered markers only if the content is actually a sequence. Four: motion serves the subject — orchestrated moments beat scattered effects. Five: complexity matches the vision — minimalism needs precision, maximalism needs execution.

props.sparkLine: Design for this brief. Not for a similar brief.

beats.narration_text: The two-pass process. First pass: a design plan with four components. Color — four to six named hex values. Type — typefaces for each role: a characterful display face used with restraint, a complementary body face, a utility face for data if needed. Layout — one-sentence descriptions and ASCII wireframes to compare options. Signature — the single element the page will be remembered by. Then critique: does any part of the plan read as the default you would produce for any similar brief? If yes, revise it and explain what you changed. Only after confirming relative uniqueness should you write the code.

props.sparkLine: Plan first. Critique second. Code third.

beats.narration_text: Restraint. Spend your boldness in one place. Let the signature be the memorable element — keep everything around it quiet. The Chanel rule: before you leave the house, look in the mirror and remove one accessory. Critique your own work as you build; take screenshots if your environment supports it. On writing in design: words appear for one reason — to help the user navigate. Write from the end user's side of the screen. Active voice by default: Save Changes, not Submit. Errors explain what went wrong and how to fix it — they don't apologize, they're never vague. Keep the register matched to the audience.

props.sparkLine: One memorable thing. Everything else quiet.

beats.act: teardown — 2-pass insight + what it gets right/bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown moment. The architectural insight of the frontend-design skill is the two-pass gate: a design plan with a critique checkpoint before any CSS is written. The plan forces a token system — color, type, layout, signature — and the critique gate asks whether any part reads as a generic default for a similar brief. What it gets right: it names and blocks the three AI design traps, it inserts a brainstorm-critique-build loop, and it instructs Claude to derive every decision from the plan. Where it bites: design quality still depends on subject interpretation; the aesthetic risk instruction has no formula; and writing quality is unconstrained by the skill.

props.sparkLine: A plan. A critique. Then code.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. The frontend-design skill is a two-pass process: design plan then critique gate before any code. The plan requires four to six hex values, typefaces for each role, a layout concept, and a signature element. Three AI defaults are named and blocked: cream-serif-terracotta, dark-acid-green, broadsheet hairline. The critique gate asks: does this read as a generic default for any similar brief? If yes, revise before building. Restraint: spend boldness once. Writing: active voice, no apology errors.

artifactLines: 2-pass process: design plan → critique gate → code (always in that order)
Token system: 4–6 hex values · typefaces per role · ASCII layout · signature
3 defaults blocked: cream+serif, dark+acid-green, broadsheet hairline
Critique gate: 'does this read as generic for any similar brief?' — revise if yes
Restraint: spend boldness in one place, keep everything else quiet
Writing: active voice, errors explain + direct, register matched to audience

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude session. Paste this: Design a landing page for a handmade ceramic studio. I want a strong visual identity — distinctive typography, a considered palette, and one design element I will not have seen on other ceramic studio sites. Use the frontend-design skill. Then watch whether Claude names a subject and audience before designing, describes a palette as hex values, and states what the signature element is before it writes any CSS. If it starts coding immediately — no plan, no critique — the two-pass gate was skipped. That is the tell.

props.command: Design a landing page for a handmade ceramic studio. Strong visual identity — distinctive typography, considered palette, one element I won't have seen elsewhere. Use the frontend-design skill.
props.runningText: Watch: does Claude name the subject? Describe hex values? State the signature before CSS?
output: Expected: subject named + audience identified before any design choices
Expected: 4–6 named hex values in the plan, not CSS variables from thin air
Red flag: CSS written before the design plan is stated — gate was skipped

beats.narration_text: Frontend Design. Liam, in for Bear.
